findings.summary: Two parallel Model base classes with incompatible interfaces undermine the entire type hierarchy
evidence: src/core/model.py Model.__init__ takes (name, parameters) with no 'case' argument and stores _case_history, _precision_weights, connections, _case_configurations
src/models/base.py Model.__init__ takes (name, case) with no 'parameters' argument and stores only data_buffer — a completely different shape
src/models/base.py Model.case setter raises TypeError on non-Case values and never calls _apply_case_transformation, meaning subclass override hooks are silently skipped
The two classes share no common base and are not in an inheritance relationship, so code written against one Model contract is silently incompatible with the other
findings.suggestion: Make src/models/base.py a thin subclass of src/core/model.Model (not a parallel class), or delete it entirely and migrate the 57+ importers. The current state makes it impossible to reason about which contract any given model satisfies.

findings.summary: InsectModel.transform_case bypasses the case property setter's _apply_case_transformation hook on assignment, creating a silent split execution path
evidence: src/core/model.py case setter (line 58-71): sets _prior_case, appends to _case_history, then calls self._apply_case_transformation()
src/models/insect/base.py InsectModel.transform_case (lines 327-362): calls _update_precision_for_case and _update_neural_priorities, then assigns self.case = target_case (line 354)

The precision update in _update_precision_for_case writes to attribute names like 'action_precision' (line 441-443) that do not exist on the model — setattr silently creates them as orphan attributes with no effect on the parent's _precision_weights dict

ActiveInferenceModel._apply_case_transformation (lines 153-180) is therefore called AFTER the custom InsectModel precision setup, meaning the parent's precision multiplication on likelihood_precision fires on potentially stale values
findings.suggestion: Override _apply_case_transformation in InsectModel and put the precision/priority logic there, so there is a single execution path through the case setter. Remove the public transform_case method or have it simply delegate to self.case = target_case.

findings.summary: Case handler classes (NominativeCase, AblativeCase, etc.) contain only @staticmethod members and carry no state, making them effectively modules masquerading as classes
evidence: NominativeCase (nominative.py): every method is @staticmethod — apply, process_update, calculate_free_energy, get_parameters
AblativeCase (ablative.py): same pattern — apply, process_update, calculate_free_energy, get_parameters, emit_to are all @staticmethod
CaseManager stores these as class objects (not instances) in self.case_handlers dict and calls handler.apply(model), handler.process_update(model, data) — no instantiation ever occurs
The single class-level PRECISION constant (e.g. NominativeCase.PRECISION = 1.5) is the only data, and it is never read by the CaseManager dispatch path
findings.suggestion: Either convert case handlers to plain module-level functions (eliminating spurious class ceremony) or make them proper stateful objects instantiated per-model so the PRECISION constant and other config can vary per instance. The current form is neither fish nor fowl.

findings.summary: ActiveInferenceModel._apply_case_transformation only mutates state for the NOMINATIVE case; all other cases are silently no-ops, making 7 of 8 case transitions effectless at the core level
evidence: Lines 153-180: _apply_case_transformation reads precision_focus from _case_configurations and only executes a branch for precision_focus == 'likelihood' (which maps exclusively to Case.NOMINATIVE per line 101)

For all other precision_focus values ('parameters', 'outputs', 'inputs', 'operations', 'contexts', 'historical', 'identity') the if-chain has no matching branch — the function still records belief_history but makes no precision adjustment
The method's docstring says 'Each case transformation adjusts parameter access, precision weighting, etc.' — this is only true for NOMINATIVE
findings.suggestion: Add elif branches for each precision_focus value, or refactor to a dispatch table keyed on precision_focus that applies the appropriate precision adjustment. At minimum, add a logger.debug call in the else path so silent no-ops are observable.
